reasoning: The Raiders' QB room has been untenable for years and Mendoza is the only passer in this class you can hand a franchise to without hedges — 3-year starter, elite arm, composure under pressure. Positional value plus a clean trait profile makes this the least complicated #1 pick on the board. You don't outsmart yourself here.

Hard disagreement with the consensus LB/off-ball trend — you take the 20-year-old EDGE with an 18-year-old breakout and 15% pressure rate. Simmons's age curve alone makes him more valuable than any LB in this class. The Jets' defensive identity reboots around a pass rusher who's five years from his athletic peak.

Arizona's defense has no centerpiece chess piece, and Perkins is the closest thing in this class to a modern Von Miller-meets-Derwin hybrid. The injury flag matters, but at pick 3 the ceiling is worth the risk. He fits every sub-package on every down.

If you're committed to Will Levis, you protect him with the cleanest LT in the class. Mauigoa broke out at 19 and has 36 starts at the highest college level — floor-plus-ceiling pick. Skill positions can wait when foundational tackle play is on the board.

Second consecutive OT on the board is fine — Fano is a near-equal prospect and the Giants' line still has gaps on both sides. With Jaxson Dart developing, protection is the leverage move. Power-scheme versatility means he can start Day 1 at either tackle spot.

The Browns need a true alpha WR1, and Tyson's production profile — 38% market share, 3.14 YPRR, contested-catch dominance — is the class's cleanest receiver resume. Scheme-versatile, polished route tree, built for volume. Whoever plays QB in Cleveland gets a #1 target on Day 1.

Jayden Daniels is already a franchise QB; you lean into defensive premium value. Bailey's hand usage and bend are the class's best after Simmons, and his 9.48 RAS makes him a scheme-free pick. Washington gets the DPOY-upside rusher their roster has lacked.

The Saints' pass rush has plateaued and Bain is a three-down EDGE with genuine power — 87 PFF grade, 15.8% pressure rate, sets the edge in the run game. You don't reach for a QB in a thin QB class. Best EDGE left at a position of pressing need.

This is the board gift of the top 10 — Downs is the highest-graded safety prospect since Derwin James with a 93 PFF grade and box/deep versatility. Spagnuolo's defense runs on interchangeable DBs. With Mahomes extended, compounding championship-level defensive pieces is the franchise's job.

Doubling back to offense for a 20-year-old with an 18-year-old breakout — Williams has the youngest runway of any WR in this class and the traits profile to match. Giants leave the top 10 with two blindside tackles and a WR1 with 10-year ceiling. This is how you rebuild an offense in a single draft.

Miami's pass rush cannot keep up with Allen and Mac in the AFC East — Faulk is an SEC-tested, 21-year-old EDGE with elite run defense and ascending pass-rush numbers. Youth plus premium position equals a 10-year starter. Tackle depth can be patched in round 2.

Dallas has papered over their CB2 spot for three years — Delane is a press-man corner with 18% forced-incompletion rate and 86 coverage grade. Quinn's defense lives on boundary corner play. Plug-and-play starter at a premium position.

Stafford's window runs on pocket integrity and Proctor is a 21-year-old LT with 25 SEC starts already logged. Frame, movement skills, pass-pro chops — you don't pass on young franchise tackles. This insulates the Rams' window for three more years.

Baltimore's interior DL has aged and Woods is a 22-year-old 3-tech with 10.8% pressure rate — interior disruption that doesn't exist in free agency. Pairing him with Madubuike resets the front for 5 years. The Ravens always value premium trench athletes.
The Bucs need a modern hybrid LB/S to match AFC/NFC offenses. Styles is a coverage-first LB with sub-package utility and a 9.40 RAS. Better value than a pure off-ball LB — he survives on every down.

After drafting Simmons at 2, the Jets lean back into offense. Tate is a polished X-receiver with 2.92 YPRR and the frame to be Garrett Wilson's opposite. Your EDGE + WR1 draft rebuilds both sides of the ball in one night.

The Lions lost Jack Campbell-adjacent depth and Reese is a hybrid LB/EDGE with 9.55 RAS and 14.8% pressure rate — he's a subsub-package weapon on top of MIKE-LB duties. Brad Holmes loves versatility premiums. This is a roster-fit home run.

Minnesota's CB room has been an annual hole and McCoy's 9.02 RAS plus 17.1% forced incompletion rate makes him a plug-and-play outside corner. Flores's defense runs on DBs who can press and blitz. This is the least flashy, most necessary pick.

Bryce Young needs a condensed-formation safety valve, and Sadiq is a 9.12-RAS movement TE who doubles as an in-line blocker. Carolina's offense has lacked a middle-of-field target for a decade. You anchor Young's reads.

Going back to the defensive well — Davis is a 6'4" press-match CB whose length is the rarest trait in this secondary class. Pair him with Delane at 12 and the Cowboys exit this draft with two rookie starters on the boundary. Premium position + premium traits.

Pittsburgh's EDGE depth behind TJ Watt needs planning, and Dennis-Sutton is a 9.16 RAS pro-ready rusher with length and power. Tomlin's defense rotates bodies — you need three starters, not two. This is a long-horizon trench investment.

Harbaugh builds through the front. Lawrence is a twitchy, 8.92-RAS EDGE with 14.1% pressure rate — the kind of G5 riser who tests through the roof. Pass rush depth behind Mack/Bosa has always been thin. Premium position at late-first value.

Philadelphia's slot/WR3 role needs a twitchy athlete who can tie together Smith and Brown. Stewart's 9.22 RAS and 19-year-old breakout signal trait ceiling that matches what Hurts currently lacks. Injury flags are real but the upside at 23 is worth it.

Pairing Lemon with Tyson (taken at 6) gives Cleveland two rookie WRs with complementary profiles — Tyson the X, Lemon the separator slot/flanker. Lemon broke out at 19, is 21 at draft, and has 2.88 YPRR. You can't have too many weapons.

Chicago needs a 1-tech anchor next to Dexter — McDonald is a wide-bodied run-stuffer with enough pass-rush utility to not be a liability. Premium position value and immediate run-defense upgrade. Caleb Williams benefits from a defense that gets off the field.

The AJ Terrell bloodline matters and Avieon is a pedigreed outside corner with smooth hips and 8.74 RAS. Buffalo needs a CB2 who can survive against KC's and Baltimore's passing games. Coverage grade + contested-catch upside = a Sean McDermott archetype.

Shanahan adores movable YAC weapons and Concepcion is the cleanest Deebo-lite prospect in the class. 19-year-old breakout, 8.94 RAS. San Francisco finds their next Deebo successor at a value that reflects his gadget ceiling.

Stroud's weapons room needs a contested-catch red-zone threat, and Boston's 59% contested-catch rate is the highest in the class. Big frame, 8.70 RAS. Houston picks the missing piece for an AFC title-contending offense.

After Downs at 9, you circle back to the OL. Miller is a 38-start RT with pro-ready pass-pro technique. Mahomes's pocket integrity is the franchise's highest-leverage protection investment. High-floor pick at a premium position.

Miami takes another OL. After Faulk at 11, you build around Tua's pocket. Freeling is an SEC-tested RT with length and above-average pass protection. You leave this draft better on both sides of the trenches.
Vrabel's defense runs on instinctive MIKEs and Allen's 85 PFF grade plus 8.60 RAS is the prototype. Georgia linebackers translate. This is a culture-setting pick the Patriots' defensive reboot needs.

Seattle closes the round with a swing on the highest-ceiling QB remaining. Allar has 40 career starts, prototype size, and 9.12 RAS — Geno Smith is a bridge, not a solution. The Seahawks get a developmental franchise passer at a fraction of the top-5 QB tax.
